chapter_summary: Reading tests are highly reliable predictors of economic and civic success because they measure the ability to communicate with strangers in the public sphere. However, the mistaken belief that reading is an abstract 'how-to' skill has led schools to waste time on ineffective drills, when the true driver of reading comprehension—and high test scores—is the acquisition of broad background knowledge.

claims.text: Scores on fill-in-the-bubble reading tests are the most reliable predictors of Americans’ future economic status and ability to become effective citizens.
claims.source_passage: In the last chapter I noted that scores on fill-in-the-bubble reading tests are the most reliable predictors of Americans’ future economic status and ability to become effective citizens.

claims.text: Reading is an act of communication with strangers who share the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: Reading is an act of communication with complete strangers who share the public sphere.

claims.text: Reading ability encompasses multiple skills including a readiness to write and speak well.
claims.source_passage: Reading ability embraces the multiple skills one needs in order to become effective in that sphere, including a readiness to write and speak well.

claims.text: Reading requires a large dimension of unwritten knowledge to understand what an absent stranger conveys.
claims.source_passage: It is a special kind of communication that necessarily makes use of a large dimension of unwritten knowledge required to understand what an absent stranger is trying to convey by words coming from a loudspeaker, printed on a page, or reproduced on a computer screen.

claims.text: The ability to understand strangers is the foundation for learning new things, prospering in school, and job training.
claims.source_passage: From the ability to understand strangers and make oneself understood in turn, other competencies flow. One can learn new things readily, prosper in school, and possess the general knowledge needed to train for a new job.

claims.text: No Child Left Behind has caused an unintended consequence of misspending school time on how-to skills and test preparation.
claims.source_passage: No Child Left Behind reasonably places a big emphasis on reading tests, but that has unfortunately accounted for the unintended consequence that much time is being misspent on how-to skills and test preparation.

claims.text: Stagnant or declining twelfth-grade reading scores indicate that schools do not understand how to help students achieve high scores.
claims.source_passage: Stagnant, even declining twelfth-grade scores on reading tests indicate that our schools do not fully understand what they must do to help students achieve high scores on these tests.

claims.text: Any state or district that follows specific principles of content-based instruction will raise its reading scores to a high international level.
claims.source_passage: I make the following guarantee: any state or district that follows the principles outlined here will raise its reading scores (and therefore the competencies of its students) to a high international level.

claims.text: Complaints that reading tests are culturally biased are unfounded.
claims.source_passage: Reading tests are attacked for cultural bias and other faults, but such complaints are unfounded.

claims.text: Standardized reading tests are fast and accurate indexes of real-world reading ability.
claims.source_passage: The tests are fast and accurate indexes of real-world reading ability.

claims.text: Reading tests correlate extremely well with actual capacity to learn and communicate.
claims.source_passage: They correlate extremely well with one another and with actual capacity to learn and communicate.

claims.text: Standardized tests are fair because the scoring conditions are regularized.
claims.source_passage: Standardized tests are thus fair. The complaints would be even more vigorous if the tests were un-standardized and therefore unfair.

claims.text: The real problem with testing is the narrowing of the curriculum, not the tests themselves.
claims.source_passage: The much more reasonable complaint is that an emphasis on testing has caused schools to devote too much time to drills and test preparation, with a consequent narrowing of the curriculum.

claims.text: Reading is not an abstract skill like typing that can be used on any text once techniques are learned.
claims.source_passage: the mistaken assumption that reading is a skill like typing and that once you know the right techniques you can read any text addressed to a general audience.

claims.text: Schools are wasting enormous amounts of time practicing drills that actually deprive students of the knowledge needed for comprehension.
claims.source_passage: The opportunity costs have been enormous. Schools are wasting hours upon hours practicing drills that are supposed to improve reading but that are actually depriving students of knowledge that could enhance their reading comprehension.

claims.text: Reading scores in later grades have declined because skill-based 'cramming' has displaced coherent content.
claims.source_passage: Ever since this cramming has been put into effect, reading scores in later grades have trended downward, because the cramming has driven out the very thing—coherent content—that could enable children to ace their reading tests.

claims.text: Test questions about 'the main idea' do not probe general skills but rather probe background knowledge of the topic.
claims.source_passage: Many educators see this question as probing the general skill of “finding the main idea.” It does not. ... Such a child, though much trained in comprehension strategies, might answer the question incorrectly. ... [The advantaged student] has the background knowledge to comprehend what the passage is saying.

claims.text: Many educators incorrectly view questions about specific text passages as probing a general skill of 'finding the main idea.'
claims.source_passage: Many educators see this question as probing the general skill of “finding the main idea.” It does not.

claims.text: A student's failure to answer a comprehension question is often caused by a lack of specific background knowledge rather than a lack of comprehension strategies.
claims.source_passage: Such a child, though much trained in comprehension strategies, might answer the question incorrectly... this student has the background knowledge to comprehend what the passage is saying.

claims.text: The research foundations claiming that practicing comprehension strategies improves reading scores are questionable.
claims.source_passage: But the research foundations for this claim are questionable, especially if one factors in opportunity cost—what schools could be doing during the same time periods that would improve reading even more.

claims.text: The Report of the National Reading Panel is weak and unreliable in its section on reading comprehension.
claims.source_passage: they are following the recommendations of the Report of the National Reading Panel, which is weak and unreliable in its section on reading comprehension.

claims.text: Subject-matter knowledge trumps formal skill in reading comprehension.
claims.source_passage: It has been shown decisively that subject-matter knowledge trumps formal skill in reading

claims.text: Proficiency in one reading-comprehension task does not necessarily predict skill in another.
claims.source_passage: proficiency in one reading-comprehension task does not necessarily predict skill in another.

claims.text: Standardized reading tests implicitly acknowledge the knowledge-dependence of reading by offering multiple passages on varied topics.
claims.source_passage: Test makers implicitly acknowledge this by offering, in a typical reading test, as many as ten passages on varied topics. (If reading were a knowledge-independent skill, a single passage would suffice.)

claims.text: Standardized reading tests do not actually test comprehension strategies, despite appearing to do so.
claims.source_passage: The key conceptual mistake is that, contrary to appearances and educators’ beliefs, these reading tests do not test comprehension strategies.

claims.text: Questions like 'What is the main idea?' probe ad hoc comprehension rather than a general technique.
claims.source_passage: There are usually questions like “What is the main idea of this passage?” but such a question probes ad hoc comprehension, not some general technique of finding the main idea.

claims.text: Reading comprehension is not a universal, repeatable skill comparable to sounding out words or athletic movements.
claims.source_passage: Reading comprehension is not a universal, repeatable skill like sounding out words or throwing a ball through a hoop.

claims.text: 'Reading skill' is an overgeneralized abstraction that obscures the reality that reading is comprised of content-constituted skills.
claims.source_passage: “Reading skill” is rather an overgeneralized abstraction that obscures what reading really is: an array of separate, content-constituted skills such as the ability to read about the Appalachian Mountains or the ability to read about the Civil War.

claims.text: Real-world reading comprehension relies on a 'mountain of taken-for-granted knowledge' in which new information is embedded.
claims.source_passage: That is exactly how a school textbook or the Internet offers new information: it is embedded in a mountain of taken-for-granted knowledge. That is the way language always works.

claims.text: Beyond a few short lessons, the teaching of reading-comprehension strategies is a waste of instructional time.
claims.source_passage: beyond a few short lessons, the teaching of reading-comprehension strategies is a waste of time.

claims.text: Understanding a text about plant domestication requires historical and agricultural knowledge that many tenth graders lack.
claims.source_passage: Ask a group of tenth graders what it means to domesticate a plant, and chances are that most will not know... and they will not understand that part of the test passage

claims.text: The domestication of plants is fundamental to human history.
claims.source_passage: Domestication of plants is fundamental to human history.

claims.text: All language communication, including school textbooks and the internet, functions by embedding new information within a 'mountain' of taken-for-granted knowledge.
claims.source_passage: it is embedded in a mountain of taken-for-granted knowledge. That is the way language always works.

claims.text: Successful performance on reading tests requires 'domain-specific knowledge' regarding the topics covered in the test passages.
claims.source_passage: To get the right answers on a reading test takes what cognitive psychologists call “domain-specific knowledge,” a principle that holds for all language communication.

claims.text: Teaching children a broad array of domain-specific knowledge is more effective for developing mature readers than practicing reading strategies like 'finding the main idea.'
claims.source_passage: it is far more fruitful to teach children the broad array of domain-specific knowledge they will need to become mature readers than to practice reading strategies such as “finding the main idea,” “clarifying,” and “summarizing.”

claims.text: The time currently spent on reading strategy exercises in schools displaces time that should be spent on acquiring critical domains of knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The vast amounts of time teachers and students currently spend on those strategy exercises is time they are not spending on domains of knowledge that are critical for understanding books, newspapers, newscasts, and the great grab bag of information that is the Internet.

claims.text: Current standardized reading tests are unfair to students who have low general knowledge due to their home or school environments.
claims.source_passage: Hence the tests are unfair to students who, through no fault of their own, have little general knowledge.

claims.text: The reading achievement gap between white and minority students is caused by a difference in background knowledge, not a difference in innate ability.
claims.source_passage: This difference in knowledge, not any difference in ability, is the fundamental reason for the reading gap between white and minority students.

claims.text: The process of gaining the general knowledge and vocabulary necessary for mature reading is inherently slow and cumulative.
claims.source_passage: We go to school for many years partly because it takes so long to build up the vast general knowledge and vocabulary we need to become mature readers.

claims.text: General reading tests can only be fair above the fifth or sixth grade, provided students have received coherent, content-based schooling in earlier grades.
claims.source_passage: Because this knowledge-gaining process is slow and cumulative, the type of general reading test now in use could be fair to all groups only above fifth or sixth grade, and only after good, coherent, content-based schooling in the previous grades.

claims.text: States should institute curriculum-based reading tests in grades one through four that contain passages based on specific knowledge received through schooling.
claims.source_passage: Let us institute curriculum-based reading tests in first, second, third, and fourth grades—that is to say, reading tests containing passages based on knowledge that children will have received directly from their schooling.

claims.text: It is impossible to administer a fair reading test in the early grades that is not curriculum-based.
claims.source_passage: In the early grades, when children are still gaining this knowledge slowly and in piecemeal fashion, it is impossible to give a fair reading test of any other sort.

claims.text: Standardized tests inevitably drive school curriculum and instruction.
claims.source_passage: Tests would continue to drive schooling—as always

claims.text: Curriculum-based testing drives schooling in an educationally productive way by systematically enhancing background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: but they would do so in an educationally productive way that systematically enhanced background knowledge.

claims.text: A high-quality knowledge-based education is the only legitimate way to prepare students for reading assessments.
claims.source_passage: Offering children a good education is the right way to cram for a reading test.

claims.text: Reading tests in each grade should contain passages from specific domains within a coherent, pre-announced curriculum.
claims.source_passage: each grade will contain passages from domains taken from that coherent curriculum

claims.text: State-mandated reading tests should be accompanied by teaching materials and professional guidance for the specific topics being tested.
claims.source_passage: At the same time that the state made this announcement it would also make available the materials and professional guidance teachers would need to teach those topics effectively.

claims.text: Listing more topics for testing than are actually used ensures that all topics in the curriculum will likely be taught.
claims.source_passage: Because more topics would be listed than would be tested, all the topics the state mentioned would likely be taught.

claims.text: Standardized tests can drive schooling in an educationally productive way if they are designed to systematically enhance background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Tests would continue to drive schooling—as always—but they would do so in an educationally productive way that systematically enhanced background knowledge.

claims.text: Providing children with a comprehensive education is the most effective method of preparing for a reading test.
claims.source_passage: Offering children a good education is the right way to cram for a reading test.

claims.text: To enable children to succeed on reading tests, schools must systematically impart the general knowledge that is assumed in writing for a general audience.
claims.source_passage: We need to impart systematically—starting in the very earliest grades by reading aloud to students, then later in sequenced self-reading—the general knowledge that is taken for granted in writing addressed to a general audience.

claims.text: Reading prowess on standard tests like the NAEP will rise dramatically in later grades if early tests are based on curriculum-specific topics.
claims.source_passage: By later grades, when the reading tests become the standard non-curriculum one, such as the NAEP tests, reading prowess will have risen dramatically.

claims.text: The anti-curriculum sentiment in current educational policy makes it difficult for states to implement curriculum-based testing.
claims.source_passage: But it seems doubtful that any state can now resist the anti-curriculum outcry that would result from actually putting curriculum-based testing into effect.

claims.text: Early grade education is significantly more critical for long-term academic success than high school reform.
claims.source_passage: The general knowledge and vocabulary required for effective learning at the high school level are slow-growing fruits of a long process. The way to reform high school is to prepare elementary school students to thrive there.

claims.text: The prevalence of watered-down subjects in high schools is a result of students lacking the foundational knowledge necessary for rigorous coursework.
claims.source_passage: If, over time, high school has become a smorgasbord of watered-down subjects, it is because watered-down subjects are all most students are now prepared to understand.

claims.text: The resistance to teaching academic subject matter is most intense in preschool and the earliest elementary grades.
claims.source_passage: The victory of the anti-curriculum movement has chiefly occurred in the crucial early grades, and the further down one goes in the grades, the more intense is the resistance to academic subject matter

claims.text: Withholding academic knowledge from poor children in early school grades is unfair, especially as wealthy children acquire such knowledge at home.
claims.source_passage: It does not seem to occur to the intellectual descendents of Rousseau that the four-year-old children of rich, highly educated parents might be gaining academic knowledge at home that is unfairly being withheld at school

claims.text: The influential 1983 report A Nation at Risk incorrectly focused on high school as the primary arena for educational improvement.
claims.source_passage: The most influential educational document of recent years, A Nation at Risk (1983), focused on high school... for most of its length, A Nation at Risk ignored the first eight grades of schooling

claims.text: The authors of A Nation at Risk erroneously assumed that the early grades should be devoted to abstract 'enabling skills' rather than specific content.
claims.source_passage: The authors of A Nation at Risk accepted the how-to view of early education that now dominates the American educational world. They assumed that any sensible content will do so long as it develops the necessary foundational skills.

claims.text: The decline in high school academic standards was a consequence of the long-term decline in elementary learning, not its cause.
claims.source_passage: But in the longer historical perspective, the watering down of high school was less a cause of lower scores than a consequence of the gradual decline of learning.

claims.text: The experts and commissioners behind 'A Nation at Risk' assumed that any sensible content would suffice as long as it developed foundational skills.
claims.source_passage: They assumed that any sensible content will do so long as it develops the necessary foundational skills.

claims.text: The path to improvement in early grades was historically seen by experts to lie in developing higher-order proficiencies rather than the substance taught.
claims.source_passage: The path to improvement is the early grades was seen to lie less in the substance that was taught than in developing higher-order proficiencies.

claims.text: American students lack critical thinking and problem-solving skills compared to students in Asian and European countries.
claims.source_passage: Yet according to international studies, these skills turn out to be precisely the ones our students lack compared with students in Asian and European countries that place less emphasis on formal skills and more on coherent year-to-year subject matter.

claims.text: Higher-order skills are not learned best by focusing on them directly in isolation.
claims.source_passage: Higher-order skills are important, but they are not learned best by endlessly focusing on them.

claims.text: Most school-based skills are knowledge-based and require domain-specific knowledge and practice.
claims.source_passage: Cognitive science is clear: through domain-specific knowledge and through practice. By the time A Nation at Risk was published in 1983, cognitive psychology had achieved a consensus about the knowledge-based character of most school-based skills and the importance of long practice.

claims.text: Educational policy currently lacks significant integration with cognitive science findings.
claims.source_passage: today there is still little crossover between cognitive science and educational policy.

claims.text: Gaining an academic skill such as reading is dependent on the specific curricular content used to teach it.
claims.source_passage: A Nation at Risk simply assumed that gaining an academic skill such as reading or reckoning is independent of the specific curricular content through which the skill is taught. This is wrong.

claims.text: High expertise in any subject is generally the result of tenacity of practice over a long duration rather than innate talent.
claims.source_passage: In general, it is not some Superman-like endowment that accounts for high expertise in any subject but rather tenacity of practice (lasting, on average, some ten years).

claims.text: Wide knowledge and large vocabulary are prerequisites for achievement in high school and result from gradual accretion.
claims.source_passage: Wide knowledge and a large vocabulary—the prerequisites to achievement in high school—are gradual accretions.

claims.text: School districts or states should institute an explicit, grade-by-grade core curriculum in grades K-8 for at least 50 percent of school time.
claims.source_passage: Institute in your district or state an explicit, grade-by-grade, core curriculum in grades K-8 occupying at least 50 percent of school time.

claims.text: States should cooperate to create common curricula to foster national educational commonality over time.
claims.source_passage: the best policy for now is for each state to institute a good elementary core curriculum, either on its own or in cooperation with other states, and to assume that a desirable tendency toward ever greater commonality among the states will emerge over time.

claims.text: Prejudices against commonality and set curricula are the primary barriers to American educational improvement.
claims.source_passage: Nevertheless, prejudices against commonality, and indeed against any set curriculum, continue to dominate American education... these inert prejudices, which still constitute perhaps our biggest barrier to educational improvement.

claims.text: The question 'Who will decide?' regarding curriculum content is a rhetorical ploy used to stop discussion rather than a valid argument.
claims.source_passage: First, how would we answer the conversation stopper 'Who will decide?'... But this is a rhetorical ploy, not an argument.

claims.text: Every high-performing and fair educational system in the world has successfully solved the problem of determining a common content core.
claims.source_passage: In fact no high-performing and fair educational system has failed to solve it.

claims.text: An American core curriculum can solve its political obstacles if it meets the criteria of acceptability and effectiveness.
claims.source_passage: If an American core curriculum can meet two criteria—acceptability and effectiveness—then the political problem can be solved, and there will be a real chance to reverse decades of American educational decline.

claims.text: Inert prejudices regarding curriculum constitute the biggest barrier to American educational improvement.
claims.source_passage: I will discuss these inert prejudices, which still constitute perhaps our biggest barrier to educational improvement.

claims.text: A common core curriculum must meet the dual criteria of acceptability and effectiveness to solve the political problem of implementation.
claims.source_passage: If an American core curriculum can meet two criteria— acceptability and effectiveness—then the political problem can be solved, and there will be a real chance to reverse decades of American educational decline.

claims.text: The American public generally supports the idea of schools teaching shared national traditions and ideals of freedom and equality.
claims.source_passage: We know from surveys that the public generally likes the idea of a common core and wants the schools to teach the traditions that hold the country together—traditions such as respect for those laws, institutions, and ideals of freedom and equality

claims.text: The core curriculum for math and science is essentially universal and uncontested across different national contexts.
claims.source_passage: Moreover, few sensible people will wish to launch a campaign against a core curriculum in math and science, which are the same in China as in Chattanooga.

claims.text: An explicit curriculum will be publicly accepted in the United States if it is proven to be highly effective in teaching reading and writing.
claims.source_passage: An explicit curriculum would be accepted in the United States if it were shown to be highly effective in imparting an ability to read, write, and learn at a high level. Effectiveness and acceptability go together in the public mind

claims.text: The content of a core curriculum is effectively decided by the existing tacit knowledge of the language community.
claims.source_passage: Hence the answer to the question “Who decides?” is “The language community has already largely decided.”

claims.text: Communally shared knowledge can be systematically inventoried and taught to children.
claims.source_passage: That communally shared knowledge can be inventoried and taught to children.

claims.text: A core curriculum that systematically imparts shared community content is the most effective way to develop reading, writing, and learning ability.
claims.source_passage: A core curriculum that systematically imparts this content will be optimally effective in developing reading, writing, and learning ability. This technical insight is the heart of the Core Knowledge movement and explains its effectiveness in raising reading and writing scores.

claims.text: Changes to the character of a community's shared knowledge must be the result of democratic debate rather than ideological imposition.
claims.source_passage: Any impulse to change the character of the community’s shared knowledge should be a matter of open, vigorous, democratic debate, not something imposed by ideologues on the poor.

claims.text: The use of a 'one-in, one-out' rule for content selection enables diverse and suspicious groups to reach consensus on a core curriculum.
claims.source_passage: The principle was this: if a group agreed to add something to a grade level, they also had to agree to take something out. . . . These practical restrictions imposed a useful discipline that made us keep in view the practicalities of teaching what is most needed to understand the written word in the American public sphere.

claims.text: A common core curriculum should only specify 50 percent of the content, leaving the remaining 50 percent to local and teacher preference.
claims.source_passage: We were all aware that in the other 50 percent of the curriculum, the unspecified half, teachers, schools, and localities could express very different preferences.

claims.text: To reach agreement on a definite curriculum core, a group must follow the principle that adding a topic to a grade level requires taking another topic out.
claims.source_passage: The principle was this: if a group agreed to add something to a grade level, they also had to agree to take something out.

claims.text: The 50 percent of the school curriculum that is not specified in the core should be left to the preference of teachers, schools, and localities.
claims.source_passage: We were all aware that in the other 50 percent of the curriculum, the unspecified half, teachers, schools, and localities could express very different preferences.

claims.text: Complaining about the arbitrariness of specific curriculum decisions is a grave intellectual error.
claims.source_passage: It is a grave intellectual error to make too much of this arbitrariness, just as it would be to complain of the decision to drive on the right side of the road rather than the left.

claims.text: All modern national knowledge assumptions are arbitrarily invented constructs.
claims.source_passage: All modern national knowledge assumptions are arbitrarily invented constructs— put together with glue and thread, as scholars like Ernest Gellner and Eric Hobsbawm have shown.

claims.text: Arbitrary cultural constructs become traditional and difficult to change once they are established in books and schools.
claims.source_passage: Once shared knowledge assumptions have been in place for a time in books and schools, they become, like the standard language, a system that is difficult to change except at the edges.

claims.text: The early school curriculum should emphasize the most stable dimensions of the language community’s shared traditions.
claims.source_passage: The early curriculum should emphasize the most stable dimensions of the language community’s shared traditions, as recorded in the books of its libraries.

claims.text: The agreed-on core curriculum should gradually evolve to reflect consensus changes regarding inclusiveness and social justice.
claims.source_passage: At the same time, in the United States, that agreed-on core should gradually come to reflect consensus changes toward greater inclusiveness and progress in social justice, which are part of our founding ideal.

claims.text: The specific knowledge required for high literacy in the United States is fairly stable.
claims.source_passage: Any effective core for American schools will implicitly follow some of the same principles, because the knowledge that is most needed for high literacy in the United States is fairly stable.

claims.text: There exists a definite body of knowledge that is taken for granted within the American language community.
claims.source_passage: There is a definite body of knowledge taken for granted in the language community.

claims.text: Future curriculum reformers must focus more on rhetorical skills than scientific research because no scientific argument against a common core exists.
claims.source_passage: Reformers will not need to sharpen their scientific wits so much as their rhetorical ones. No one has produced a scientifically acceptable argument against commonality in a core curriculum.

claims.text: A common core curriculum does not cause everyone to think alike or produce 'cookie-cutter' sameness.
claims.source_passage: For example, it is not difficult to refute the claim that “cookie-cutter" sameness causes everyone to think alike.

claims.text: The primary requirement for improving American schools is an intellectual revolution in public opinion rather than just structural changes like charter schools.
claims.source_passage: The chief need for improving American schools, private and public, regular and charter, is an intellectual revolution in public opinion.

claims.text: An academic curriculum is considered an intolerable heresy by the dominant 'anti-curriculum creed' in American education.
claims.source_passage: But I have come to understand that an academic curriculum is the one heresy the anti-curriculum creed cannot tolerate.

claims.text: A powerful system of enforced intellectual conformity exists within American schools of education and their professional meetings.
claims.source_passage: The power of enforced intellectual conformity within the education world can be understood only by those who have attended the annual meeting of education professors or enrolled in an American school of education.

claims.text: Teachers are often socially and professionally pressured to reject Core Knowledge as reactionary and old-fashioned.
claims.source_passage: Teachers in Nashville were induced to say they would refuse to teach this reactionary, old-fashioned stuff. Social pressures were brought to bear against Core Knowledge sympathizers

claims.text: Many methods and theories taught in education schools regarding socio-cultural approaches lack empirical evidence.
claims.source_passage: I became disillusioned with methods and theories of teaching and learning that seemed to lack empirical evidence and simply did not resonate as being sensible.

claims.text: Education professors frequently prioritize children 'doing' authentic activities over the acquisition of disciplined content knowledge.
claims.source_passage: I have sat through lecture after lecture where I have heard Professors of Education suggest that children should be “doing” “authentic” science/math/ social studies etc. as opposed to acquiring a strong content knowledge of these discipline areas

claims.text: Needy children suffer most when self-serving educational ideologies permeate mainstream discourse and practice.
claims.source_passage: I feel that if the ideologies that I now see as purely self-serving are allowed to permeate into the discourse and practice of mainstream education, then American children, particularly the most in need of the efficacious power of a solid education, will suffer greatly.

claims.text: Questioning progressive educational concepts like 'community of learners' leads to being labeled as an oppressor or bigot.
claims.source_passage: My experience, however, is that as soon as I question such laudable phrases as “community of learners,” “reciprocal teaching,” “peer review,” or “authentic learning,” I am dismissed as at best a hopeless conservative and at worst as an “oppressor” or bigot.

claims.text: American colleges of education function like sectarian churches that crush heresy rather than like academic departments that encourage debate.
claims.source_passage: In an earlier chapter I noted that American colleges of education are like sectarian churches where heresy is crushed rather than like university departments where debate is encouraged.

claims.text: University leaders should intervene in education schools to ensure that pro-curriculum perspectives are represented.
claims.source_passage: We need university provosts or presidents who will insist that the education schools appoint a few pro-curriculum professors to counterbalance the anti-curriculum monopoly

claims.text: University psychology departments are generally critical of the 'ideological psychology' taught in colleges of education.
claims.source_passage: especially departments of psychology, whose members are, as a rule, less than complimentary about the ideological psychology taught in colleges of education.

claims.text: The educational establishment uses 'anti-tyranny' rhetoric to oppose citizens' initiatives for core curricula.
claims.source_passage: But the entire local educational establishment rose up against these citizens, placing hyperbolic anti-tyranny ads in the newspaper.

claims.text: Questions of educational content are often falsely portrayed as partisan political issues rather than pedagogical ones.
claims.source_passage: the question of content was portrayed as a partisan issue, with Republicans for lists, Democrats against them.

claims.text: The educational establishment claims that 'learning how to learn' is more important than specific content due to the pace of technological change.
claims.source_passage: The amendment’s methods and materials fail to teach “learning how to learn,” a skill that becomes increasingly necessary as technology moves us ever faster into the future.

claims.text: The debate over educational content is frequently portrayed as a partisan issue, with Republicans supporting specific lists and Democrats opposing them.
claims.source_passage: In the public arena of Texas, as in other states, where battle lines are drawn between Republicans and Democrats, the question of content was portrayed as a partisan issue, with Republicans for lists, Democrats against them.

claims.text: Mainstream educational groups characterize the inclusion of specific content lists in state standards as 'education regression' and 'rote memorization.'
claims.source_passage: The amendment, written by a narrow interest group and questionable in its validity ...is a sterling example of “rote memorization and parroting back facts.” It is not education reform—it is education regression.

claims.text: Research does not support the claim that successful teachers focus on process rather than content.
claims.source_passage: Research has shown time and again that process is what successful teachers teach, and what unsuccessful teachers don’t teach. [This is exactly the opposite of what research shows. EDH]

claims.text: The 'student-centered classroom' model relies on the teacher serving as a coach or role model while students engage in active analysis and innovation.
claims.source_passage: The commissioned TEKS assumes a student-centered classroom, where the teacher creates or designs a learning situation and the students actively engage in their learning: consuming information, thinking, questioning, analyzing, Innovating, creating. The teacher then serves as role model, support or coach

claims.text: Specific content lists are often rejected on the grounds that they fail to meet the needs of a diverse student population who need to 'see themselves' in stories.
claims.source_passage: First, it fails to take into account the needs of a diverse student population with a suggested reading list that is unnecessarily limited in its range. Children who are not yet accomplished and sophisticated readers want and need to see themselves in stories.

claims.text: Social and political pressure from education professionals is strong enough to resist efforts to insert specific guidance into state standards.
claims.source_passage: Teachers and administrators turned out in force to denounce the idea of inserting specific guidance into the state standards—exerting a social and political pressure that the board could not resist.

claims.text: The grand architecture of American public schools expresses a quasi-religious commitment to equality and community.
claims.source_passage: Why design all these expensive steps and high columns if not to express an almost religious devotion to equality and community through education?

claims.text: The founding ideal of the American public education system was to create a meritocracy based on talent and virtue rather than birth.
claims.source_passage: The message of such architecture is clear: by educating our citizenry we would create a great nation. It would be a meritocracy—as Jefferson put it, an aristocracy of virtue and talent, not of birth.

claims.text: Public education should allow children of all economic backgrounds to compete as equals based on industry and intellect.
claims.source_passage: Let the children of the rich and the poor take their seats together and know of no distinction save that of industry, good conduct and intellect

claims.text: The New York schools in the 1920s and 1930s delivered a good education to all students and functioned very well.
claims.source_passage: The schools functioned very well in the 1920s. There were plenty of kids who left to go to work, but no one pushed them out. Every community had good or good-enough schools.

claims.text: In the 1920s and 1930s, schools possessed a curriculum that was universally understood by the community.
claims.source_passage: There was a curriculum, which everyone understood.

claims.text: By the 1940s, progressive education in America began replacing factual study and multiplication tables with 'learning by doing.'
claims.source_passage: The announcer triumphantly states that in tens of thousands of schools across America, children are no longer memorizing the multiplication table or pursuing dull, bookish studies. They are learning by doing.

claims.text: By the 1960s, the home environment replaced the school as the primary determinant of an American child's educational success.
claims.source_passage: By the 1950s, and with blossoming fullness in the 1960s, as the Coleman Report has shown, it was the home and not the school that determined how well educated an American child would become.

claims.text: Attributing recent American educational decline to the influx of Hispanic and Black students is a logical fallacy (post hoc ergo propter hoc).
claims.source_passage: Apologists tend to view our recent educational decline as having been caused by an influx of Hispanic and black students, thus committing the logical fallacy known as post hoc ergo propter hoc

claims.text: The decline in American educational performance was caused by the disappearance of the academic curriculum, which happened to coincide with demographic shifts.
claims.source_passage: My alternative explanation is that the influx of Hispanic and black students in the post-desegregation era coincided tragically with the decline of the academic curriculum in the public schools.

claims.text: History of textbook contents provides evidence for the decline of the academic curriculum in public schools.
claims.source_passage: The history of the contents of our textbooks supports this thesis.

claims.text: When a school fails to provide a coherent academic curriculum, only children from 'home-enriched' environments can succeed academically.
claims.source_passage: Once the academic curriculum had disappeared, no student, rich or poor, who grew up outside an enriched home environment could (except for the odd voracious reader) expect to become well educated. When a school ceases to offer a coherent academic curriculum, only a home-enriched child can thrive academically.

claims.text: A coherent, cumulative curriculum based on knowledge—rather than 'how-to' drill—is the only way to improve student achievement.
claims.source_passage: a coherent, cumulative curriculum—knowledge, not how-to drill—is the road to improved student achievement.

claims.text: Current state educational standards are fundamentally flawed because they lack clear and specific content.
claims.source_passage: American Educator came out with an issue entitled There’s a Gaping Hole in State Standards: What’s Missing? Clear, Specific Content.

claims.text: The educational establishment functions as an intellectual monopoly that is too entrenched to reform itself.
claims.source_passage: The ability of the educational establishment to resist pro-content ideas had been a remarkable case study in intellectual monopoly within a free society.

claims.text: Strong external pressure from politicians and citizens is required to reform schools and colleges of education.
claims.source_passage: Strong external pressure on schools and on colleges of education by a better-informed set of politicians and citizens is required.

claims.text: The United States has a greater need for a common core curriculum in early grades than other nations because of high population mobility.
claims.source_passage: The need for a common core curriculum in the early grades is far greater in the United States than in other nations that actually have one. Americans move from one place to another in greater numbers than do residents of any other country.

claims.text: As a transethnic nation, the United States requires an invented common public sphere based on shared traditions rather than ancestry.
claims.source_passage: As a transethnic nation, we have a greater need for an invented common public sphere that is determined not by blood and soil or hearth and home but by transethnic traditions concerning our history, laws, and freedoms.

claims.text: Language in the public sphere cannot be disentangled from specific, commonly shared knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The medium of this public sphere is language, which cannot be disentangled from specific, commonly shared knowledge.

claims.text: Unifying national knowledge can only be learned in community-centered schools because it is not available in diverse American homes.
claims.source_passage: That unifying knowledge cannot be learned in our hugely diverse homes, as it is in more homogeneous countries. It can only be learned in our community-centered schools.

claims.text: The association of the anti-curriculum movement with the Democratic Party is an accident of history rather than a logical necessity.
claims.source_passage: The connection of the anti-curriculum movement with the Democratic Party is an accident of history, not a logical necessity.

claims.text: A definite core curriculum in the early grades is a necessary requirement for achieving the liberal goal of social justice.
claims.source_passage: A dominant liberal aim is social justice, and a definite core curriculum in early grades is necessary to achieve it.

claims.text: Practical improvement of public education requires the depolarization of the curriculum issue and cooperation between the political left and right.
claims.source_passage: Practical improvement of our public education will require intellectual clarity and a depolarization of this issue. Left and right must get together on the principle of common content.

claims.text: The child-centered educational movement has weakened American competence, solidarity, and equality of opportunity.
claims.source_passage: The apparently benign idea of natural, child-centered education that took hold at the beginning of the twentieth century came by gradual degrees to vitiate our public education, weaken our county’s competence and competitiveness, diminish our solidarity, and destroy equality of opportunity.

claims.text: Nothing of consequence can be done to save public education until the anti-curriculum theory is rejected.
claims.source_passage: Nothing of consequence can be done to save public education until the disastrous anti-curriculum theory, which has dominated American schooling for nearly a century, is vigorously set aside.

claims.text: The 'making of Americans' must once again become the dominant purpose of the public school.
claims.source_passage: The making of Americans that these thinkers viewed as the dominant purpose of the public school must be made dominant once again, enriched by the humane traditions of pedagogical practice that the child-centered movement introduced.

claims.text: The complaints against standardized tests would be even more vigorous if the tests were un-standardized and therefore unfair.
claims.source_passage: The complaints would be even more vigorous if the tests were un-standardized and therefore unfair.

claims.text: The authors of A Nation at Risk cannot be faulted for their 'how-to' view of early education because they were reflecting the unanimous consensus of the experts they consulted.
claims.source_passage: One cannot fault them for this. How could they think otherwise when there was unanimity among experts?

claims.text: A small initial advantage in math ability creates a feedback loop of pleasure and practice that eventually produces mathematical expertise.
claims.source_passage: They begin with a small basic advantage in math ability that leads them to take pleasure in math. Doing problems and practicing calculations is a rewarding activity for them, and they practice math more and more.

claims.text: University provosts and presidents must intervene in education schools to ensure that pro-curriculum professors are appointed to counterbalance the existing anti-curriculum monopoly.
claims.source_passage: We need university provosts or presidents who will insist that the education schools appoint a few pro-curriculum professors to counterbalance the anti-curriculum monopoly

claims.text: University authorities must establish mechanisms for students in education schools to lodge complaints when they are victims of intellectual tyranny.
claims.source_passage: who will make sure that students victimized by intellectual tyranny can lodge complaints with university authorities

claims.text: Decisions regarding curriculum content are analogous to traffic laws, like driving on the right side of the road; the specific choice is less important than the existence of a shared agreement.
claims.source_passage: It is a grave intellectual error to make too much of this arbitrariness, just as it would be to complain of the decision to drive on the right side of the road rather than the left.

claims.text: The goals of early American thinkers like Thomas Jefferson and Benjamin Rush regarding the 'making of Americans' should be integrated with the humane pedagogical practices introduced by the child-centered movement.
claims.source_passage: The making of Americans that these thinkers viewed as the dominant purpose of the public school must be made dominant once again, enriched by the humane traditions of pedagogical practice that the child-centered movement introduced.

claims.text: The watering down of high school curricula was a consequence of the decline in student learning over time, rather than its primary cause.
claims.source_passage: But in the longer historical perspective, the watering down of high school was less a cause of lower scores than a consequence of the gradual decline of learning.

claims.text: Eighty-four percent of parents believe children should learn about American political institutions, history, and ideals of freedom and equality.
claims.source_passage: In the Public Agenda report A Lot to Be Thankful For, 84 percent of parents said they wanted their children to learn about America’s political institutions, history, and ideals of freedom and equality.

claims.text: The early curriculum should focus on the most stable dimensions of shared traditions while allowing the core to evolve slowly toward greater social justice and inclusiveness.
claims.source_passage: The early curriculum should emphasize the most stable dimensions of the language community’s shared traditions... At the same time, in the United States, that agreed-on core should gradually come to reflect consensus changes toward greater inclusiveness and progress in social justice

evidence.description: The decline or stagnation of twelfth-grade reading test scores.
Linda Perlstein's documentation of how formal reading techniques on vapid texts have displaced history, science, and the arts.
An actual passage from a New York State 4th-grade reading test about the Appalachian Trail.
A contrast between a disadvantaged fourth grader and an advantaged one answering a question about the Appalachian Trail.
The observation that standardized tests include up to ten varied passages to ensure reliability.
Analysis of a tenth-grade Florida state test passage on the history of cotton and plant domestication.
The use of a passage from Kant's Critique of Pure Reason as a mock reading test to show that strategy cannot overcome knowledge gaps.
The principle of 'domain-specific knowledge' as established in the field of cognitive psychology.
A proposed state announcement listing specific core curriculum domains (e.g., Greek myths, speed of light, Canada) to illustrate how tests could drive knowledge acquisition.
The Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation spent hundreds of millions on high school reform with disappointing results.
The 1983 report 'A Nation at Risk' focused primarily on high school curriculum fragmentation and declining scores while ignoring early grade content.
A 1982 hearing entitled 'Language and Literacy' featured expert testimony that emphasized integrated, concept-oriented language skills rather than specific domain knowledge.
The 1982 'Language and Literacy' hearing where 22 speakers emphasized higher-order skills over content.
International studies showing US students lack higher-order skills compared to students in countries with subject-matter-focused curricula.
Cognitive psychology consensus that skills are knowledge-based and require domain-specific practice.
Stanislas Dehaene's research in 'The Number Sense' regarding the development of mathematical expertise through practice.
The existence of common content cores in other successful multicultural liberal democracies.
Public Agenda report showing 84% of parents want schools to teach American political institutions and ideals of freedom.
evidence.source_reference: Public Agenda report, A Lot to Be Thankful For

evidence.description: General reference to high-performing multicultural liberal democracies having solved the common curriculum problem.
The 1990 Charlottesville conference where 120 diverse participants reached agreement on a K-6 content sequence.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch's personal account of Core Knowledge Sequence development

evidence.description: The 'add-one-take-one' principle allowed diverse, suspicious groups in the 1990s to successfully agree on a core curriculum.
Fierce disagreements and eccentricity in countries with core curricula like France and Finland disprove the 'cookie-cutter' effect.
The failure of Mayor Phil Bredesen's Core Knowledge initiative in Nashville due to resistance from education professors and specialists.
The K-2 history and geography thread of the Core Knowledge Sequence illustrates the cumulative effect of a genuine curriculum.
The failure of the Bredesen initiative in Nashville due to local teacher resistance despite a large conference attendance.
An email from a PhD student in Curriculum Theory describing the hostile environment for dissenting views in ed schools.
The case of a northern Illinois superintendent who stole his rival's signs to prevent the implementation of Core Knowledge.
The failure of the Hawaii statewide Core Knowledge campaign despite the success of a local public school and business support.
The Texas state school board debate where a list of suggested reading works was attacked as 'regression' and 'rote memorization.'
E-mailed petition to the Texas state board by a teachers' group opposing the inclusion of specific reading lists.
The 2008 public defeat of the Texas State Board of Education's attempt to add specific content examples to state standards.
Architecture of Venable Elementary School in Charlottesville (1925), featuring expensive steps and high columns.
The New York State Education Building in Albany (1908), designed by Henry Hornbostel with thirty-six tall columns.
Edwin Blashfield’s mural 'The Graduate' in the Great Hall of CCNY, depicting a student transformed into a citizen by education.
Historian Diane Ravitch's expert testimony regarding the functionality and clear curriculum of New York schools in the 1920s.
A 1940s 'March of Time' film documenting the shift toward 'learning by doing' and away from rote factual learning in tens of thousands of schools.
The Coleman Report, which found that home environment was the primary predictor of student achievement.
Historical analysis of American textbook contents showing a decline in academic rigor over time.
The American Educator magazine's publication of cognitive science research advocating for a cumulative, knowledge-based curriculum.
The American Federation of Teachers (AFT) publication American Educator has published numerous articles connecting cognitive science to cumulative curriculum and achievement.
A specific issue of American Educator entitled 'There’s a Gaping Hole in State Standards: What’s Missing? Clear, Specific Content.'
The recent formation of the organization 'Common Core' which includes a bipartisan coalition advocating for common content.
Texas teachers denouncing specific state standards as socially unjust and teacher-unfriendly.

concepts.author_definition: A condition where scoring is regularized so that different versions of a test yield consistent results for students of similar ability, ensuring fairness.
concepts.source_passage: Most people think the word refers to a soulless, fill-in-the-bubble format, but the term actually means that scoring conditions have been regularized so that any version of the test given on any occasion will yield approximately the same score for students of similar reading abilities.

concepts.author_definition: An act of communication with strangers in the public sphere that relies heavily on unwritten background knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: An abstract, transferable skill similar to typing.
concepts.source_passage: Reading is an act of communication with complete strangers who share the public sphere. ... It is a special kind of communication that necessarily makes use of a large dimension of unwritten knowledge

concepts.author_definition: The specific understanding of a particular passage achieved through the interaction of general literacy and specific background knowledge about that passage's topic.
The idea that what we call 'reading skill' is actually a collection of domain-specific abilities to understand texts about particular subjects.
The tacit background information that writers assume readers already possess, which is necessary to understand new information in a text.
The specific factual information and context required to understand a particular text or domain of communication.
concepts.common_alternative: General intelligence or abstract reading skill.

concepts.author_definition: A testing model where reading passages are drawn from a pre-announced, coherent set of subject-matter domains (e.g., history, science, myths) taught in the school curriculum.
The educational ideology that resists teaching academic subject matter in early grades, often rooted in Rousseauvian naturalism.
Abstract foundational skills like reading, writing, and 'reckoning' that are often prioritized in early education over factual content knowledge.
Skills like comprehension, composition, critical thinking, and problem solving, which the author argues are not independent traits but the result of domain-specific knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: General, transferable thinking strategies that can be taught independently of factual content.

concepts.author_definition: The requirement of roughly ten years of persistent effort and study within a specific subject to achieve high expertise.
The degree to which the public supports a curriculum based on its alignment with shared traditions and proven efficacy.
The ability of a curriculum to raise student performance in reading, writing, and learning by imparting shared background knowledge.
The broad group of speakers and citizens whose shared tacit knowledge forms the necessary basis for successful communication and literacy.
The 50 percent of school time not dictated by a common core curriculum, reserved for local, school, or teacher autonomy.
A practical restriction in curriculum design where any addition of a topic requires the removal of another to maintain focus and feasibility.
Arbitrary cultural constructs that, over time and through institutionalization in schools and books, become stable systems of shared knowledge.
The dominant educational orthodoxy, particularly in ed schools, that resists the implementation of a specific, grade-by-grade academic core.
A slogan used by education professors to emphasize 'doing' the work of a discipline rather than learning its established facts.
concepts.common_alternative: Learning through real-world application and engagement.

concepts.author_definition: The specific psychological theories taught in education schools that align with progressive ideology but are often rejected by academic psychology departments.
A process-oriented skill favored by modern educational theorists as a primary goal of schooling, supposed to transcend specific subject matter.
concepts.common_alternative: The author views this as a flawed concept that ignores the necessity of domain-specific knowledge.
concepts.source_passage: The amendment’s methods and materials fail to teach “learning how to learn,” a skill that becomes increasingly necessary as technology moves us ever faster into the future.

concepts.author_definition: A pedagogical model where students actively engage in information consumption and analysis while the teacher acts as a coach rather than a primary source of knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: Opposed to 'teacher-centered' classrooms where information is directly transmitted.
concepts.source_passage: The commissioned TEKS assumes a student-centered classroom, where the teacher creates or designs a learning situation and the students actively engage in their learning

concepts.author_definition: A Jeffersonian ideal of meritocracy where social status is determined by individual merit and education rather than inherited wealth or birth.
concepts.source_passage: It would be a meritocracy—as Jefferson put it, an aristocracy of virtue and talent, not of birth.

concepts.author_definition: A progressive pedagogical approach that replaces factual memorization and bookish study with active, physical projects like mock stores or field trips.
concepts.common_alternative: Hands-on learning or experiential education, often viewed positively by modern educators as more engaging than rote learning.
concepts.source_passage: The announcer triumphantly states that in tens of thousands of schools across America, children are no longer memorizing the multiplication table... They are learning by doing.

concepts.author_definition: A student who receives a wealth of academic knowledge and vocabulary from their family life, allowing them to succeed even when the school fails to provide a curriculum.
concepts.source_passage: When a school ceases to offer a coherent academic curriculum, only a home-enriched child can thrive academically.

concepts.author_definition: A large-scale challenge to the dominant 'received ideas' and scientifically misleading rhetoric in the educational world by informed citizens and policy makers.
concepts.source_passage: The chief need for improving American schools... is an intellectual revolution in public opinion.

concepts.author_definition: The state of the educational establishment where it is collectively resistant to pro-content ideas and reform despite being in a free society.
A nation like the United States where unity is based on shared traditions, history, and laws rather than shared ethnicity (blood and soil).
The primary civic purpose of public education: to create unified citizens from diverse backgrounds through shared knowledge and values.
The century-long dominant educational theory in America that favors 'natural' child-centered approaches over a specific core curriculum.
Shared history, laws, and freedoms that unify a diverse population beyond blood, soil, or ancestry.
A condition within schools of education where student dissent against progressive 'how-to' dogmas is suppressed through social and professional labeling.
The 'one-in, one-out' rule used to reach curriculum consensus: if a new topic is added, an existing one must be removed to maintain coherence and feasibility.
The process by which arbitrarily chosen constructs of shared knowledge become stable and traditional over time through libraries and schooling.

cases.name: New York State 4th-grade reading test
cases.description: A specific reading assessment containing a passage about the Appalachian Trail used to demonstrate that 'main idea' questions actually test geographical and cultural background knowledge rather than abstract comprehension skills.
key_passages: There is a path that starts in Maine and ends in Georgia, 2,167 miles later. This path is called the Appalachian Trail. ... Many educators see this question as probing the general skill of “finding the main idea.” It does not.

cases.name: Florida Tenth-Grade State Test
cases.description: A state-level reading assessment featuring a passage from National Geographic about the origins of cotton. The author uses this case to show how the test assumes knowledge of geography (Indus Valley vs. Peru), agriculture (bales), and history (domestication).
key_passages: Here is a passage from a tenth-grade Florida state test: The origin of cotton is something of a mystery...
The writer of this passage (which was, the state of Florida informs us, taken from National Geographic) clearly expected his readers to know what cotton is and what plant domestication is.

cases.name: Education Week Kant Experiment
cases.description: Hirsch published a short piece in Education Week using a notoriously difficult passage from Immanuel Kant to challenge the idea that reading strategies (summarizing, finding the main idea) allow comprehension without background knowledge. He argued that even expert adults (educators) could not correctly identify the main idea of the passage unless they understood the philosophical problem Kant was solving.
key_passages: I hit on the idea of presenting a mock reading test containing a passage chosen completely at random from one of the most influential books ever written—Immanuel Kant’s notoriously difficult Critique of Pure Reason.
I offered the readers of Education Week lots of time to apply the strategies they compel children to practice: summarize, classify, and find the main idea. ... Most, I suspect, were still clueless.
contestable_aspects: The representativeness of Kant's prose for general reading assessments
The assumption that educators failed the mock test (Hirsch only 'suspects' they were clueless)

cases.name: A Nation at Risk (1983)
cases.description: A seminal educational report that highlighted the decline in American educational quality but focused almost exclusively on high school reforms and fragmented curricula while accepting the skill-based 'how-to' model for early grades.
key_passages: The most influential educational document of recent years, A Nation at Risk (1983), focused on high school
The authors of A Nation at Risk accepted the how-to view of early education that now dominates the American educational world.

cases.name: Asian and European educational systems
cases.description: Nations that place less emphasis on formal skills and more on coherent year-to-year subject matter, resulting in higher student performance in critical thinking and problem solving.
key_passages: students in Asian and European countries that place less emphasis on formal skills and more on coherent year-to-year subject matter

cases.name: High-performing multicultural liberal democracies
cases.description: Nations that have successfully implemented common curricula despite having diverse populations, showing that the political problem of 'who decides' is solvable.
key_passages: The problem has been solved in other multicultural liberal democracies. In fact no high-performing and fair educational system has failed to solve it.

cases.name: The 1990 Core Knowledge Sequence Conference
cases.description: A working conference held in Charlottesville, Virginia, involving 120 people from diverse organizations and interest groups. Participants worked in small tables to review a draft K-6 sequence, following a discipline where adding content required removing other content. This process successfully moved a group characterized by 'mutual suspicion' toward a consensus 'band of brothers and sisters.'
key_passages: In 1990, we held a working conference. Some 120 people, representing different organizations and interest groups... met in Charlottesville, Virginia, for several days to amend this document.
Despite the intensity of identity politics in the 1980s and 1990s, a mundane practical principle enabled this diverse group of people to reach agreement on a definite core.
The principle was this: if a group agreed to add something to a grade level, they also had to agree to take something out.

cases.name: Nashville/Bredesen Reform Effort
cases.description: In the late 1990s/early 2000s, Nashville Mayor Phil Bredesen attempted to implement Core Knowledge citywide. Despite support from the teachers' union and superintendent, education professors at Vanderbilt's Peabody School and curriculum specialists led an uprising, inducing teachers to refuse the 'reactionary' curriculum and successfully killing the initiative.
key_passages: Some years ago, the mayor of Nashville, the Democrat Phil Bredesen... offered financial inducements to the various interest groups to make Core Knowledge the citywide curriculum.

But when they returned to Nashville, the curriculum specialists and the education professors of the Peabody School of Education at Vanderbilt University rose up against this challenge to their ideas and expertise.
Social pressures were brought to bear against Core Knowledge sympathizers, and the Bredesen idea died a quick death.

cases.name: Nashville Core Knowledge Initiative
cases.description: Mayor Phil Bredesen attempted to introduce Core Knowledge, but teachers and the local educational establishment resisted it as 'reactionary,' eventually causing the initiative to fail despite high teacher interest elsewhere.
key_passages: Teachers in Nashville were induced to say they would refuse to teach this reactionary, old-fashioned stuff.
A few years later, when we held an annual Core Knowledge conference in Nashville... not one teacher from Nashville attended.

cases.name: Northern Illinois Citizens Initiative
cases.description: A group of parents and citizens pushed for a core curriculum, backed by cognitive scientists. They were met with hyperbolic 'anti-tyranny' ads and illegal tactics by the incumbent superintendent who successfully fought off the reform.
key_passages: The incumbent superintendent was even discovered stealing his Core Knowledge rival’s vote-for-me signs and carting them away in his car.

cases.name: Hawaii Statewide Curriculum Reform
cases.description: Business leaders led by David Rolf attempted to adopt Core Knowledge statewide in Hawaii (a low-performing state). Despite success in a local school and legislative interest, a newspaper firestorm killed the movement.
key_passages: Hawaiian schools remain as they were.
the newspaper firestorm that ensued was too intense for any business leader or legislator to withstand.

cases.name: Texas Language-Arts Standards Debate
cases.description: An attempt to add specific text examples to state standards was portrayed as a partisan Republican vs. Democrat issue, with opponents arguing it would lead to 'rote memorization' and fail diverse students.
key_passages: Because the state school board was split between Republicans and Democrats, the question of content was portrayed as a partisan issue

cases.name: Texas TEKS Controversy 2008
cases.description: An attempt by the Texas State Board of Education to add specific illustrative examples (like 'The Emperor's New Clothes') to the state standards was met with fierce opposition from a teachers' group. The opposition argued that such lists promoted rote memorization and were insensitive to diversity. The proposal was ultimately defeated by social and political pressure from education professionals.
key_passages: In fact, the constructive effort by the state board was roundly defeated at a public meeting in the spring of 2008.
Teachers and administrators turned out in force to denounce the idea of inserting specific guidance into the state standards—exerting a social and political pressure that the board could not resist.
contestable_aspects: The characterization of the teacher opposition as being against 'mere listing of illustrative examples' versus substantive pedagogical concerns.

cases.name: The Free Academy (City College of New York)
cases.description: Founded in 1847 by Townsend Harris, this institution was designed to educate the rich and poor together, focusing on industry and intellect. Its architecture, specifically Shepard Hall, was designed like a cathedral to symbolize the sanctity of the educational mission to create citizens.
key_passages: Let the children of the rich and the poor take their seats together and know of no distinction save that of industry, good conduct and intellect
It depicts a young man being transformed into a shining citizen by what seems to be the goddess of education

cases.name: The 'March of Time' 1940s film
cases.description: A media example used to illustrate the 'triumphant' rejection of bookish learning in favor of activities like mock stores and airport visits, marking the transition into progressive pedagogical dominance.
key_passages: The movie shows children doing everything except learning the facts of history and math. They are buying and selling items in mock stores, pushing toy trains, and taking field trips.

cases.name: American Federation of Teachers (AFT)
cases.description: Under the tradition of Al Shanker and through its magazine American Educator, this union has advocated for core content and national interests, even when it appeared to conflict with short-term teacher interests.
key_passages: One of the two teachers’ unions, the American Federation of Teachers, has made a beginning... its successors at American Educator have carried on that tradition.

cases.name: The Organization 'Common Core'
cases.description: A new bipartisan organization representing a shift in the intellectual landscape, bringing together Democrats, Republicans, and union representatives to advocate for shared content.
key_passages: A beginning is being made with a new organization called Common Core that includes Democrats, Republicans, and representatives of the American Federation of Teachers.

thinkers.name: Linda Perlstein
thinkers.key_idea: Formal reading techniques and 'vapid readings' have displaced science, history, and arts in the classroom.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Perlstein's documentation to support his claim that the focus on test preparation has narrowed the curriculum and harmed students' education.
key_passages: Linda Perlstein has documented how the enforced practice of formal techniques conducted on vapid readings has displaced time that young children could and should spend on history, science, and the arts.

thinkers.name: Immanuel Kant
thinkers.key_idea: The dense and difficult nature of his philosophical prose in 'Critique of Pure Reason'.
thinkers.engagement: The author uses Kant's text as a 'limit case' to prove that no amount of reading strategy can facilitate comprehension if the reader lacks the necessary philosophical background knowledge.
key_passages: I hit on the idea of presenting a mock reading test containing a passage chosen completely at random from one of the most influential books ever written—Immanuel Kant’s notoriously difficult Critique of Pure Reason.

thinkers.name: Franz Kafka
thinkers.key_idea: The character Josef K's struggle against a mysterious, illogical authority in 'The Trial'.
thinkers.engagement: The author compares the plight of teachers and students under the 'how-to' conception of reading to the absurdity of Kafka's protagonist.
key_passages: Operating under the how-to conception of reading, the schools find themselves in a Kafkaesque situation in relation to reading tests. Like Josef K in The Trial, teachers and students are dutifully trying to obey all the instructions... but they have still not managed to fulfill the mysterious requirements of the test.

thinkers.name: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
thinkers.key_idea: The idea that early childhood should be free from academic instruction, which Hirsch characterizes as naturalism.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch argues that the 'intellectual descendants' of Rousseau are unfairly withholding academic knowledge from poor children under the guise of noble intentions.
key_passages: It does not seem to occur to the intellectual descendents of Rousseau that the four-year-old children of rich, highly educated parents might be gaining academic knowledge at home

thinkers.name: Stanislas Dehaene
thinkers.key_idea: Mathematical expertise is developed through pleasure in the activity leading to long-term practice, rather than just innate ability.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Dehaene's observation about math expertise as a 'general application to most higher-order skills,' including language.
key_passages: In his superb book The Number Sense, Stanislas Dehaene makes an observation about mathematical expertise that has general application to most higher-order skills.

thinkers.name: Abraham Lincoln
thinkers.key_idea: Schools should promote respect for laws, institutions, and ideals of freedom and equality to preserve the union.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Lincoln as a foundational authority for the 'acceptability' of teaching shared national traditions.
key_passages: ideals of freedom and equality that Abraham Lincoln exhorted American schools to promote in order to preserve the union as the 'last best hope of earth.' Lincoln’s view is seconded by most citizens.

thinkers.name: Ernest Gellner
thinkers.key_idea: Modern national knowledge assumptions are arbitrarily invented constructs.
thinkers.engagement: Uses Gellner's scholarship to validate the idea that while curriculum is 'arbitrary,' it is a necessary functional construct for modern nations.
key_passages: All modern national knowledge assumptions are arbitrarily invented constructs— put together with glue and thread, as scholars like Ernest Gellner and Eric Hobsbawm have shown.

thinkers.name: Eric Hobsbawm
thinkers.key_idea: The Invention of Tradition: traditions are often constructs that become established over time.
thinkers.engagement: Cites Hobsbawm's work to argue that the 'arbitrariness' of curriculum is not a valid reason to reject it, as all traditions start as inventions.
key_passages: Over time these arbitrary constructs become traditional. (Hobsbawm’s book on the subject is called The Invention of Tradition.)

thinkers.name: Anonymous PhD Student
thinkers.key_idea: The socio-cultural approaches in curriculum theory lack empirical evidence and those who question them are socially ostracized in academia.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses the student's email to corroborate his claim that education schools enforce intellectual conformity and suppress dissent.
key_passages: I am dismissed as at best a hopeless conservative and at worst as an “oppressor” or bigot.

thinkers.name: Thomas Jefferson
thinkers.key_idea: The creation of an 'aristocracy of virtue and talent' through public education to replace an aristocracy of birth.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Jefferson's phrasing to define the meritocratic mission of the American common school.
key_passages: It would be a meritocracy—as Jefferson put it, an aristocracy of virtue and talent, not of birth.
Jefferson, who wished to 'avail the commonwealth of those talents and virtues which nature has sown as liberally among the poor as rich.'

thinkers.name: Townsend Harris
thinkers.key_idea: Education should be a space where rich and poor children are distinguished only by their conduct and intellect.
